title: Urolithiasis — Medical Evaluation and Stone Composition
subtitle: Block C — Stone epidemiology, pathophysiology, metabolic evaluation, medical expulsive and preventive therapy
guidelines_version: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2024 · AUA/Endourology Society Stone Guidelines 2016 (updated 2022)

learning_objectives: Describe the epidemiology, risk factors, and composition of renal and ureteral stones
Apply EAU 2024 metabolic evaluation criteria to first-time and recurrent stone formers
Select evidence-based medical expulsive therapy and understand indications for intervention
Recommend specific dietary and pharmacological preventive therapy based on stone composition
Recognise stone types associated with systemic disease and hereditary conditions

sections.title: Epidemiology, Risk Factors, and Stone Composition

blocks.content:
Urolithiasis affects 10–12% of men and 6–7% of women over their lifetime in industrialised countries, with recurrence rates of 40–50% within 5 years without preventive therapy and 70–80% within 10 years. Incidence is increasing, attributed to rising obesity, type 2 diabetes, dietary changes (increased protein, sodium, oxalate; decreased fluid intake), and improved detection rates. Men are affected more than women (2–3:1 ratio), though the gender gap is narrowing. Peak incidence is in the 4th–6th decades. Recurrence risk is highest in: metabolic syndrome, familial stone disease, anatomical abnormalities (horseshoe kidney, medullary sponge kidney, UPJ obstruction), and bowel disease (Crohn's, short bowel syndrome, bypass surgery).

**Stone composition** determines aetiology, investigation strategy, and preventive therapy. The EAU 2024 guidelines recommend stone composition analysis for ALL stones — by direct stone analysis (infrared spectroscopy or X-ray diffraction) rather than chemical analysis. Composition of urinary calculi:

**Calcium oxalate (CaOx)** stones represent 70–80% of all stones. Types: CaOx monohydrate (whewellite) — harder, more dense, associated with hyperoxaluria; CaOx dihydrate (weddellite) — more radiopaque, associated with hypercalciuria. Both are radiopaque on plain radiograph.

**Calcium phosphate (CaP)** stones represent 5–10%. Types: hydroxyapatite (associated with distal renal tubular acidosis, hyperparathyroidism); brushite (calcium hydrogen phosphate dihydrate — associated with high urinary pH, highly recurrent). Radiopaque.

**Uric acid** stones represent 5–10%. Radiolucent on plain radiograph (do NOT contain calcium). Associated with low urinary pH (<5.5), hyperuricosuria, metabolic syndrome, gout, and high protein intake. Uniquely amenable to medical dissolution with urinary alkalinisation.

**Struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate)** stones represent 5–10%. Associated exclusively with urease-producing bacteria (Proteus, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, Providencia). Grow rapidly, fill the renal collecting system (staghorn calculi). Radiopaque. Treatment: complete stone removal essential + antibiotics; if untreated → progressive renal damage.

**Cystine** stones represent 1–2%. Result from cystinuria (autosomal recessive — SLC3A1/SLC7A9 mutations). Poorly radiopaque (ground-glass appearance). Patients first present in childhood/young adulthood. Require specific therapy: high fluid intake, urinary alkalinisation, D-penicillamine or tiopronin.

blocks.title: Urinary Stone Composition: Properties, Radiological Appearance, and Aetiology
rows: Low urine pH, gout, metabolic syndrome

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Radiolucent Stone = Uric Acid Until Proven Otherwise
blocks.content: On plain radiograph (KUB), uric acid stones are radiolucent (invisible) because they contain no calcium. On CT, they appear with low Hounsfield units (200–500 HU) compared to calcium stones (>600 HU). A radiolucent filling defect on IVP/CT urogram in an obese, hyperuricaemic, or gout patient = uric acid stone. Key treatment: urinary alkalinisation (potassium citrate, sodium bicarbonate) to pH 7.0–7.2 can dissolve uric acid stones completely — the ONLY stone type amenable to medical dissolution.

Stone formation requires **supersaturation** — a state where the ionic product of constituent ions exceeds the thermodynamic solubility product (Ksp), leading to nucleation and crystal growth. The key factors determining supersaturation are: urinary concentration of stone-forming ions (calcium, oxalate, urate, phosphate, cystine), urinary volume (concentrated urine = higher supersaturation), urinary pH (affects ionisation and solubility of stone constituents), and inhibitor levels.

**Urinary inhibitors** prevent crystallisation and aggregation of crystals: citrate (most clinically important — chelates calcium, directly inhibits crystal nucleation and growth, promotes dissolution); osteopontin; Tamm-Horsfall protein (uromodulin); pyrophosphate; nephrocalcin; and crystal dissolution factors. **Hypocitraturia** (urinary citrate <320 mg/day women, <450 mg/day men) is found in 15–60% of calcium stone formers and is the most treatable risk factor — supplemented with potassium citrate.

**Hypercalciuria** (urinary calcium >250 mg/day women, >300 mg/day men, or >4 mg/kg/day either sex) is the most common metabolic abnormality in calcium stone formers, present in 40–60%. Three pathophysiological mechanisms: (1) **Absorptive hypercalciuria** — increased intestinal calcium absorption with normal serum calcium, normal PTH, elevated urinary calcium; most common type. (2) **Renal hypercalciuria** — primary defect in renal tubular calcium reabsorption → compensatory secondary hyperparathyroidism, low bone density. (3) **Resorptive hypercalciuria** — from primary hyperparathyroidism — serum calcium elevated, serum PTH elevated, hypercalcaemia with hypercalciuria and hypophosphataemia.

**Hyperoxaluria** (urinary oxalate >40 mg/day): (1) **Primary hyperoxaluria types 1, 2, 3** (genetic enzyme defects — PH1 AGXT gene, PH2 GRHPR, PH3 HOGA1) — severe, early-onset, progressive renal failure, requires specific management (PH1: pyridoxine in responders; lumasiran, approved 2020, siRNA targeting LDHA, reduces urinary oxalate by >80%); (2) **Enteric hyperoxaluria** — fat malabsorption (Crohn's, ileal resection, bariatric surgery) causes excess unbound oxalate to be absorbed in the colon with reduced urinary calcium binding — treatment: low-oxalate diet, oral calcium supplementation with meals; (3) **Dietary hyperoxaluria** — excess oxalate intake (spinach, rhubarb, nuts, tea, chocolate) in susceptible individuals.

**Uric acid lithiasis** mechanism: low urinary pH (typically <5.5) is the primary driver — uric acid is poorly soluble in acid urine but becomes soluble at pH >6.5. Hyperuricosuria (>800 mg/day men, >750 mg/day women) amplifies risk. Associated with metabolic syndrome through insulin resistance causing defective renal ammoniagenesis (reduced NH3/NH4+ buffering) leading to acidic urine independent of hyperuricaemia.

blocks.title: Critical Warning — Primary Hyperparathyroidism as Stone Cause
blocks.content: In any calcium stone former, serum calcium must be measured to exclude primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT). PHPT causes: hypercalcaemia + hypercalciuria + hypophosphataemia + elevated PTH + stones (often recurrent, mixed calcium oxalate + calcium phosphate). Treatment of PHPT (parathyroidectomy) cures the metabolic abnormality and dramatically reduces stone recurrence. Missing PHPT in a stone former is a serious diagnostic error.

The **EAU 2024 metabolic evaluation** framework distinguishes between **first-time** stone formers (low risk — minimum evaluation) and **recurrent/high-risk** stone formers (full metabolic workup). Risk factors that justify full evaluation after first stone: family history of stones, children (<18 years), bilateral stones, multiple stones, history of bowel disease, metabolic syndrome/obesity/diabetes, gout, osteoporosis, nephrocalcinosis, medullary sponge kidney, single kidney, renal impairment, recurrence despite conservative measures.

**Minimum evaluation (all stone formers)**:
- Stone composition analysis (infrared spectroscopy preferred)
- Serum: creatinine, calcium, uric acid, sodium, potassium
- Urinalysis: pH, specific gravity, crystals, infection screen
- 24-hour urine (ideally ×2 on usual diet): volume, calcium, oxalate, uric acid, citrate, creatinine, sodium, phosphate, pH

**Extended evaluation (high-risk/recurrent formers)**:
- Intact PTH (if hypercalcaemia or hypercalciuria with calcium phosphate stones)
- Vitamin D (25-OH)
- Urine cystine (if cystinuria suspected — young, bilateral, family history)
- Thyroid function, fasting glucose, HbA1c (metabolic syndrome screening)
- Renal tubular acidosis evaluation (if distal RTA suspected — calcium phosphate stones, alkaline urine, nephrocalcinosis, hypercitraturia)

Target 24-hour urine values for normal stone risk: volume >2 L/day, calcium <250 mg/day, oxalate <45 mg/day, urate <750 mg/day (men), >700 mg/day (women), citrate >550 mg/day, urine pH 5.8–6.5 (calcium stones), 7.0–7.2 (uric acid prevention/dissolution).

blocks.title: Metabolic Evaluation: Abnormalities and Targeted Management
rows: Urine Ca >300 mg/day men, >250 mg/day women
Thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg BD or indapamide 2.5 mg OD)
Potassium citrate 20–60 mEq/day in divided doses
Oral calcium carbonate with meals + low-oxalate diet
Potassium citrate (alkalinise urine); allopurinol if hyperuricosuria
Elevated serum Ca, PTH, urine Ca; low phosphate
Urine cystine >400 mg/day; urinary cystine crystals
High fluid (>3L/day), K-citrate, D-penicillamine or tiopronin
Alkaline urine pH, low urine citrate, nephrocalcinosis
Potassium citrate — corrects acidosis + increases urine citrate

blocks.title: Board Pearl — First-Time vs Recurrent Stone Former
blocks.content: EAU 2024: first-time stone former without risk factors = minimum evaluation (serum calcium, creatinine, uric acid + 24h urine). Recurrent stone former (≥2 stones), or ANY first-time former with risk factors (family history, bilateral stones, children, metabolic syndrome, bowel disease, renal impairment) = FULL metabolic evaluation including 24h urine ×2 and extended serum panel. Stone composition analysis should be performed on EVERY stone specimen.

sections.title: Acute Renal Colic: Assessment and Medical Expulsive Therapy

blocks.content:
**Acute ureteral colic** presents with sudden-onset, severe, colicky flank pain radiating to the groin, ipsilateral haematuria (90%), nausea and vomiting, and inability to find a comfortable position (distinguishes from peritonitis — patients with renal colic are restless). Haematuria, while common, is absent in 10% of cases. Urgent assessment is required to identify obstructive uropathy with infection (sepsis) — the only absolute emergency indication for immediate drainage.

Diagnostic imaging: **non-contrast CT of the kidneys, ureters and bladder (NCCT KUB)** is the gold standard for acute ureteral colic: sensitivity 95–98%, specificity 96–98%, radiation dose 2–4 mSv. NCCT identifies stone position, size (key for spontaneous passage prediction), degree of hydronephrosis, and alternative diagnoses (appendicitis, aortic aneurysm). **Ultrasound** is the first choice in children, pregnant women, and when radiation exposure minimisation is paramount — identifies hydronephrosis but less sensitive for ureteral stones. **Plain radiograph (KUB)** has limited sensitivity (45–60%) and is inadequate as the sole investigation. **CT stone attenuation** (Hounsfield units) predicts fragility for SWL: stones <900 HU are more fragile; stones >1000 HU (especially brushite and calcium oxalate monohydrate) are harder and may require endoscopic treatment.

**Spontaneous passage rates** (EAU 2024): <5 mm stones pass spontaneously in 80–90% within 4 weeks; 5–10 mm stones in 40–60%; >10 mm stones rarely pass spontaneously. Distal ureteral stones have higher spontaneous passage rates than proximal stones of equivalent size. EAU 2024 supports observation (with analgesia) for up to 4 weeks for stones ≤10 mm in the absence of infection, obstruction of solitary kidney, uncontrolled pain, or renal impairment.

**Pain management in acute colic**: NSAIDs (ketorolac IV, diclofenac suppository) are superior to opioids for analgesia in ureteral colic (superior pain reduction, less nausea/vomiting) in the absence of contraindications (renal impairment, peptic ulcer, pregnancy). Intravenous paracetamol is an effective adjunct. Opioids are used when NSAIDs are contraindicated or ineffective.

**Medical expulsive therapy (MET)**: alpha-1 adrenoceptor blockers (primarily **tamsulosin 0.4 mg OD**) relax ureteral smooth muscle (particularly distal ureter and vesicoureteric junction, which express alpha-1D/alpha-1A receptors predominantly). SUSPEND Trial (NEJM 2015, Pickard et al.): tamsulosin versus placebo for distal ureteral stones — no significant difference in passage rates at 4 weeks (passage rate 80% vs 76%). The STONE PLUS study (Lancet 2015, Furyk et al.) also found no benefit. EAU 2024: MET with alpha-blocker is recommended (weak recommendation) for ureteral stones 5–10 mm based on meta-analyses showing modest benefit in this size range, despite neutral large RCTs. If MET is used, review at 4 weeks — intervention if stone has not passed.

blocks.title: Critical Warning — Ureteral Stone with Sepsis: Urgent Drainage
blocks.content: Obstruction with concurrent urinary infection (fever, leucocytosis, positive urinalysis, ureteral stone on imaging) = infected obstructed kidney = urological emergency. IMMEDIATE decompression required — either retrograde ureteral stenting (under antibiotic cover) or percutaneous nephrostomy. Definitive stone treatment (ureteroscopy, SWL) must be deferred until sepsis is controlled. Attempting ureteroscopy through an infected, obstructed system risks urosepsis and death. Antibiotics alone without drainage are insufficient.

**General dietary recommendations** (EAU 2024, applicable to all calcium stone formers): (1) **Fluid intake**: target urine output >2 L/day, achieved with fluid intake >2.5 L/day. Urine-specific gravity target <1.010. All fluid types are acceptable, but plain water preferred; citrus juices (lemon, orange) may increase urinary citrate. **Avoid dehydration** — this is the most important single intervention reducing stone recurrence by 50–60%. (2) **Dietary calcium**: Counterintuitively, LOW calcium diet increases stone risk — reduced intestinal calcium allows more oxalate absorption from the gut, increasing urinary oxalate. Target normal calcium intake 1000–1200 mg/day from dietary sources (NOT supplements, which increase urinary calcium without the oxalate-binding benefit). The Bonn Risk Index (urinary calcium × oxalate product/inhibitor) confirms this mechanism. (3) **Dietary sodium restriction**: sodium drives calcium excretion through the proximal tubule — target <6 g NaCl/day (<2.3 g sodium). (4) **Dietary protein**: animal protein increases urinary calcium, uric acid, and oxalate while reducing urinary citrate and pH — target <0.8–1 g/kg/day of animal protein. (5) **Oxalate restriction**: limit high-oxalate foods (spinach, rhubarb, nuts, chocolate, tea) for patients with hyperoxaluria.

**Pharmacological prevention** (composition-specific): Calcium oxalate with hypercalciuria: **thiazide diuretics** (hydrochlorothiazide 25–50 mg BD, or indapamide 2.5 mg OD) — reduce urinary calcium reabsorption through enhanced distal tubular calcium reabsorption secondary to volume contraction; reduce stone recurrence by 40–50%. Add potassium citrate to prevent hypokalaemia-induced hypocitraturia. **Uric acid stones**: **potassium citrate** 20–60 mEq/day to alkalinise urine to pH 7.0–7.2 — prevents formation and can dissolve existing stones. If hyperuricosuria, add **allopurinol** (xanthine oxidase inhibitor) 100–300 mg/day. Avoid sodium bicarbonate (increases urinary sodium). **Cystine stones**: high fluid intake (>3 L/day to achieve urine cystine <250 mg/L), urinary alkalinisation with potassium citrate to pH ≥7.5, **D-penicillamine** (binds cystine, forms soluble mixed disulfide) or **tiopronin** (better tolerated than D-penicillamine) for cases refractory to fluid and alkalinisation.
